observations.recommendation:
We commended the Acting MT for ensuring the intact and regular deposit of
collections. However, we recommended the management to instruct the Acting MT to:
a) ensure collecting officers at the Municipal Treasury Office (MTO) and at
different field locations remit funds/collections to the LO/Acting MT completely and
promptly specifically, at the close of each business day, and at least once a week or as
soon as the collections reach ₱5,000.00, respectively, to prevent the possible risk of
misappropriation or loss;
b) require all collecting officers at the MTO to (i) accomplish RCD on a daily basis
and submit the same together with their collections to the LO/Acting MT at the close of
each business day; and (ii) submit individual RAAFs every end of the month for better
monitoring and observe proper accountability for Accountable Forms to prevent
mishandling of these forms; and
c) prepare the Consolidated Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms
(CRAAF) to consolidate the RAAF of AOs and submit the consolidated report
accompanied by the individual reports to the COA Auditor for verification not later than
the 5th day of the ensuing month.

We further recommended that the Human Resource Management Officer
(HRMO) evaluate the MTO’s current workforce and recommend to Management the
creation of permanent positions to eliminate the reliance on JO personnel for collection
duties, which should be handled by regular Plantilla personnel.

We recommended that Management instruct the:
a) MA and the Acting MT to (i) submit an inquiry letter to the depository bank
to request possible corrections/adjustments for the bank reconciling items; (ii)
obtain from the bank the necessary documentation such as bank debit and
credit advice, to serve as the basis for adjustments in the books of accounts;
and (iii) conduct regular reconciliation of accounting ledgers and cashbooks,
at least on a quarterly basis, to promptly identify and resolve discrepancies,
if any;
b) MA to (i) adjust stale and cancelled checks amounting ₱76,081.30 and
₱4,000.00, respectively, to correct balances of the CIB-LCCA and the
corresponding payable account; and (ii) record valid book reconciling items
in the books of accounts, duly substantiated with appropriate documentation
such as copies of DMs and CMs; and
c) Acting MT to (i) locate the payees of the staled checks and inform them of the
check status to facilitate the necessary actions for accounts adjustments; ii)
send written notice to the concerned payees at least one month before a check
becomes stale and ensure proper cancellation and reporting of stale checks;
and (iii) record the identified reconciling items in the cashbook.

We recommended that Management undertake the following courses of
action:
a) instruct the Acting MT to (i) provide the MA with a duly certified list of
taxpayers indicating the names and corresponding amounts due and
collectible for the year, which serves as the official basis for setting up RPT
and SET receivables; (ii) ensure the regular updating of the RPTAR to
incorporate revised assessed values with the year of reassessment,
corresponding tax dues, cancellations due to property transfers, updated
property details, names of new owners, and new tax declaration numbers to
maintain the accuracy and completeness of records; (iii) send demand letters
to the delinquent accounts; and (iv) exert effort to retrieve copies of the
original ORs from the taxpayers to substantiate the falsification of documents
committed by the collecting officer;
b) direct the MA to make use of the Certified List of Taxpayers and Taxes Due
to be submitted by the Acting MT as the basis for setting up the RPT/SET
Receivables and Deferred Credits at the beginning of the year;
c) direct the MA and the Acting MT to jointly ensure that the ending balances of
the RPT and SET receivable accounts are regularly reconciled with the List of
Delinquent Taxpayers. This will promote the accuracy of financial records,
enhance internal consistency, and support more reliable financial reporting;
d) strengthen the internal control system over cash management to safeguard
cash from loss, misuse or misappropriation;
e) continue to verify and confirm with the taxpayers the accuracy of details of
ORs issued by the former collecting officer, particularly those issued for CYs
2021-2023, to ensure that no other taxpayers were affected by the improper
actions of the collector;
f) require the former collecting officer to refund the total amount of ₱460,365.60,
representing unremitted collections for RPT; and
g) initiate the filing of appropriate administrative, civil and criminal actions
against the former collecting officer in connection with the falsification of ORs.

We recommended that Management to instruct the:
a) MA to (i) derecognize inventory items in the books that have already been
consumed or distributed to end-users; and (ii) maintain complete inventory
records such as SLC and SPLC;
b) Acting MT and/or Supply Officer to (i) prepare and submit the SSMI, RSPI,
RIS, ICS and CRAAF to the MAO for adjustments in the books of
consumed/issued inventories; (ii) support the issuance of semi-expendable
property to end-users with ICS to establish accountability over the items; and
(iii) maintain complete inventory records such as stock cards, SPC and
RegSPI;
c) MA and the Supply Officer to verify and reconcile the unaccounted balance of
office supplies in CY 2022, amounting to ₱634,811.61 and prepare the
necessary reports/adjustments; and
d) immediately constitute an Inventory Committee, which shall conduct the
physical count on all inventories, including the semi-expendable properties;
and prepare and submit the corresponding RPCIs and RPCSPs thereon.

We recommended that Management direct the:
a) MA and Acting MT and/or Property Custodian to (i) consider undertaking the
one-time cleansing of PPE account balances to improve the reliability of the
PPE accounts in both accounting records and property records, thereby
facilitating more effective reconciliation between the two offices; and (ii)
ensure that the PPE records are updated in a timely manner, including PCs
and PPELCs to ensure proper control, monitoring, and accountability over
the agency’s property and equipment;
b) MA to (i) provide depreciation for all depreciable PPEs owned by the
Municipality; and (ii) record the adjusting entries to correct the PPE accounts,
as follows:

c) Property Custodian to coordinate with the suppliers for the status of
registration of the three-wheeled motorcycle and transfer of vehicle ownership
of the ambulance to avoid incurring penalties, fines, or sanctions from the
LTO and possible denial or delay on insurance claims.

We recommended that Management take the following actions to address the
identified deficiencies:
a) direct the MA to coordinate with the HRMO to verify the accuracy of leave
credits earned by employees and prepare the necessary JEV as the initial setup
of the accounting entries to properly recognize the monetary value of the total
earned leave credits of officials and employees, as illustrated below:
Particulars Dr. Cr.
Prior Period Adjustment ₱19,887,371.07
Leave Benefits Payable – Current ₱ 4,131,727.88
Leave Benefits Payable – Non-Current ₱15,755,643.19
b) instruct the HRMO to prioritize the review, updating, reconciliation, and
correction of leave card balances identified in the audit, and double-check the
computation of the leave credits to enhance the accuracy and integrity of
employee leave data;
c) enjoin the HRMO and the Department Heads to conduct regular
reconciliations to improve the process of maintaining employee leave cards
and to strengthen internal controls, thereby preventing future discrepancies
and ensuring accuracy of leave records; and
d) require the Municipal Budget Officer to set aside additional reserve funds for
the accumulated compensated leave credits as of year-end to ensure adequate
financial provisions and avoid potential budgetary shortfalls in the future.

action:
a) instruct the MA to draw the following adjusting entries to correct the

b) direct the Acting MT and the MA to facilitate the transfer of funds (i) from
GF to LDRRM TF for the unexpended balances of LDRRMF for CY 2024
amounting to ₱3,217,041.30; and (ii) from TF to LDRRM TF for the balance
of STF as of December 31, 2024, amounting to ₱250,629.49.

We recommended that Management instruct the:
a) MA to (i) trace the records/documents relative to the unaccounted balance of
Due to NGAs account amounting to ₱300,204.69, reconcile the same, and
return any unutilized funds for completed/cancelled projects to the SAs; and
ii) ensure account for the revenues from NGAs in the TF books by recognizing
Subsidy from the National Government after the fulfillment of the conditions
or purpose stipulated in the agreement pursuant to Sections 44 to 47 of IPSAS
23; and
b) Acting MT to return the unutilized fund transfer for the BuB-funded project
in CY 2017 amounting to ₱748,000.00 to the SA.

We recommended that Management instruct the:
a) Planning Officer and the Municipal Engineer properly plan and closely
monitor the implementation of the development projects to ensure the timely
and orderly execution thereof; and
b) Municipal Engineer to coordinate with the contractors to ensure appropriate
corrective measures on the defects noted on the quarantine facility and
tourism outpost are performed.

We commended Management for its substantial compliance with the
established headcount requirements for JO and COS personnel, as well as for
maintaining complete supporting documents for salary and wage claims. However,
we recommended that Management require the HRMO to formulate and issue formal
policy guidelines on the recruitment and engagement of JO and COS personnel to
ensure that hiring is necessary, proper, and consistent with the Municipality’s
approved programs and projects, thereby limiting expenditures to areas where such
services are truly needed.

We recommended Management to instruct the GFPS to (a) strive for the full
implementation of GAD-related PPAs by utilizing judiciously the budget set aside for
said purposes; and (b) support the GAD AR with required documents to provide the
basis of measuring the performance of the LGU in addressing gender-related issues.

We recommended Management to instruct the LSB to (a) furnish the
concerned departments a copy of SEF Budgets and ensure that the date of transmittal
is indicated in the receiving copy on file; and (b) submit the quarterly and annual
reports using the SEF Budget Accountability Form No. 1 (Annex B) on SEF
utilization, as prepared by the MA, to the DepEd CO, through appropriate channels,
with a copy furnished to the local sanggunian concerned and Regional Offices of the
DBM and DILG.

We recommended that Management advise the MA to (a) consistently comply
with BIR rules and regulations on the withholding and remittance of taxes on
compensation, purchases, and contracts; and (b) verify and reconcile the balance of
Due to BIR account and immediately effect the necessary adjustments and/or
remittance to BIR thereafter.

We recommended that Management direct the MA to (a) ensure that all
withheld premium contributions and loan amortizations are fully and promptly
remitted; and (b) reconcile and trace the prior year’s balance of ₱119,306.17 for
proper disposition.

We commended the Management for the prompt remittance of PhilHealth
premium contributions, demonstrating compliance with R.A. No. 10606. However,
we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the MA to follow up the request
for waiver of penalties from PhilHealth and remit the premium contributions
amounting to ₱613.64 immediately.

We recommended that Management exhaust all available remedies provided
by law and regulations in enforcing the immediate settlement of the audit
disallowances totaling ₱11,431,027.19.

We recommended that Municipal Mayor:
a) instruct the MA to (i) draw JEVs based on the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued to derecognize in the books the cost of supplies and materials which were already consumed and/or distributed to end-users amounting to ₱1,569,896.41 by debiting Prior Period Adjustment account and crediting Inventory accounts; and (ii) maintain supplies ledger cards for all inventory accounts to support the recorded balances of inventory; and
b) require the MT and/or Supply Officer to (i) prepare and submit the SSMI together with the original copy of the Requisition and Issue Slip to the MA monthly for issued inventories based on actual consumption/issuance for accurate recording of expenses and assets accounts for the period; and (ii) prepare the stock cards for each type of supplies to record all receipts and issuances; and
c) direct the Inventory concerned department/units particularly the Rural Health Unit, the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Office and the Agriculture Office to immediately submit to the MT and/or Supply Officer the necessary report/list of inventory items that were already distributed/issued using the RIS for proper recording in the SSMI; and
d) constitute an Inventory Committee who shall promptly and regularly conduct the semestral physical count of all Inventories of the Municipality and prepare the corresponding Report on Physical Count of Inventories thereon.
observations.managementAction: The consumed inventories costing ₱1,569,896.41 was already recognized in the books. Supplies ledger cards were not yet maintained. SSMI was partially prepared by the Property Officer. Inventory reports for items already distributed to concerned departments were already prepared. Inventory Committee was not yet constituted.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Municipal Mayor:
a) instruct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and the Municipal Engineer together with the Local Development Council and end-users to properly plan the programs/projects/activities (PPAs) to be included in the 20% Local Development Plan, and/or immediately act on realignment of funds, if needed, so that the project will be implemented during the year; and
b) consider hiring additional engineers or technical personnel to help with the preparation of detailed engineering and Program of Works (POW), if necessary, to avoid unjustifiable delays in the implementation of development projects.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2023, 12 Implemented out of 28 PPAs were not implemented. Delay in preparation of POW was again due to limited manpower in the Municipal Engineering Office with technical capabilities in planning and project development.
observations.reason: There is a need to hire/additional technical personnel in the Municipal Engineering Office.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council to:
a) monitor and evaluate the utilization of LDRRMF to ensure the full implementation of the planned activities; and
b) sustain the current effective responses to disasters and its attendant risks.
observations.managementAction: Some PPAs implemented under LDRRM Investment Plan for CY 2023 were not fully implemented. Various PPAs were implemented to effectively respond to disasters.

The LGU GAD Code was approved on September 26, 2023 through Municipal Ordinance No. 08, Series of 2023. The sex-disaggregated data obtained from the implementation of the Community-Based Monitoring System project funded out of the LDRRMF in CY 2023, will be used as additional inputs for GAD planning and budgeting. For CY 2023, the LGU attributed its PPAs to GPB without conducting gender analysis using the HGDG tool because they had not undergone yet seminars and training on the use of the said tool. The LGU implemented 57 out of 62 GAD PPAs, or 91.94%.
observations.reason: Training on the HGDG tool was not yet conducted; modules and schedule constraints affected some PPAs implementation.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a) Acting MT to (i) continue to monitor the receivables by sending demand letters on delinquent accounts and intensify efforts on collection to increase RPT and SEF collection efficiency; (ii) consider availing all legal and administrative remedies provided in R.A. No. 7160 either by administrative action through a levy on real property or judicial action as a last resort; and (iii) furnish the MA of a duly certified list showing the names of taxpayers and the amounts due and collectible for the year to serve as the basis of recording; and
b) MA to (i) secure the Certified List of Taxpayers and Taxes Due from the Acting MT to have a concrete basis for setting up the RPT/SET Receivables and Deferred Credits at the beginning of the year; and (ii) reconcile the recorded receivables with the amount actually collectible from each taxpayer based on the certified list of collectibles as reflected in the taxpayer’s ledger and adjust the books accordingly.
observations.managementAction: Demand letters were sent to delinquent taxpayers. Legal and administrative remedies were not yet availed. There is a need to update first the list of delinquencies to be based on the updated Real Property Tax Account Register (RPTAR). Reconciliation of recorded receivables with the amount actually collectible was not yet completely done.
observations.reason: Need to update the Real Property Tax Account Register and complete reconciliation.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Municipal Mayor:
a) advise the MA and the Acting MT to continually comply with the prompt remittance of withholding taxes; and
b) instruct the personnel in charge to (i) reconcile regularly the Due to BIR account and effect necessary adjustments immediately for any discrepancy noted; (ii) draw the journal entries to correct the balance of Due to BIR account and other related accounts; (iii) include in the succeeding remittance the unremitted withholding taxes of ₱768.49 as at year-end; and (iv) communicate with the BIR for possible courses of action regarding over remittance of ₱23,622.28.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality substantially complied with remittance of taxes withheld within the prescribed period. The Due to BIR account balance as of December 31, 2023 amounting to ₱128,774.71 was not yet reconciled. Adjusting entries were already booked up. The MAO already communicated with the BIR regarding the over remittance.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that Municipal Mayor exhaust all available remedies provided by law and regulations in enforcing the immediate settlement of the audit disallowances totaling ₱11,431,027.19.
observations.managementAction: Not implemented. No settlement of audit suspensions, disallowances and charges were made during the year. Appeals and prior actions were undertaken previously; some appeals were denied while one appeal remained pending at the COA Central Office.

observations.recommendation:
1.22 We recommended that Management to instruct:
a) the MA and the Acting MT to (i) conduct periodic reconciliation of their
records and take up the necessary adjusting entries, as appropriate; (ii)
exert diligent efforts to obtain from the bank the required documentation
like bank debit and credit advice as a basis in making the necessary
adjustments in the books; and (iii) coordinate with the depository bank for
possible correction/adjustment of the bank reconciling items amounting to
₱70,548.13 (net);
b) the MA to (i) make necessary adjustments to record the stale checks
totaling ₱198,098.95 by debiting CIB-LCCA and crediting the
corresponding Liabilities account; and (ii) prepare the BRS regularly and
promptly adjust valid reconciling items, as appropriate; and
c) the Acting MT to (i) return the unutilized fund transfer for the BuB funded
project in CY 2017 amounting to ₱748,000.00 to the SA; (ii) transfer the
remaining balance of STF-BuB account to GF amounting to ₱606.59
representing the interest earned for CYs 2022 and 2023; and (iii) facilitate
the closing of STF-BuB account.

1.23 The MA and the Acting MT acknowledged the audit observations and committed
to comply with the audit recommendations. During the exit conference, they commented
that the unidentified reconciling items will be communicated to the bank and they will
adjust the books accordingly.

2.13 We recommended that Management:
a) in coordination with the MA, ensure that a CA will be granted only after
the liquidation of previous one as evidenced by a Certification from the
latter that the previous CA is already settled/accounted for;
b) indicate in the SDOs appointment letters the purpose of CAs to be granted;
and
c) instruct the MA, the Acting MT, the SDOs and other concerned officials
and employees to strictly comply with the regulations on the granting,
utilization and liquidation of CAs.

3.11 We commended the Management for its substantial compliance with the
provisions of COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND JC No. 2015-01 relative to the granting
of CA for CF. However, we recommended that the MA continue to record the CA for
CF under the Advances to Special Disbursing Officer account to avoid misstatement
of Receivable accounts.

4.11 We recommended that Management instruct the:
a) MA to (i) prepare supplies ledger cards for all inventory accounts; and (ii)
draw the Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) to derecognize the cost of
inventories which were already consumed/issued to end-users totaling
₱7,441,909.46; and
b) Acting MT and/or Supply Officer to (i) prepare and submit the SSMI
together with the original copy of the Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS) to
the MA monthly for issued inventories; (ii) prepare the stock cards for each
type of supplies to record all receipts and issuances; and (iii) conduct
periodic inventory of supplies and prepare and submit the corresponding
RCPI to the AT.

5.44 We recommended that Management:
a) enjoin the MA to (i) ensure that the infrastructure projects are recorded to
appropriate Asset accounts upon completion; (ii) update the PPELC for all
property accounts to facilitate the reconciliation of property records
between MAO and MTO; and (iii) take up the necessary adjusting entries
to correct the PPE accounts;
b) direct the Acting MT and/or Property Custodian to (i) maintain complete
and updated PCs per category of PPE; (ii) continue to reconcile the
property records with the MAO, particularly the unserviceable properties,
to facilitate the disposal of items; and (iii) register and insure all the motor
vehicles under the name of the Municipality in the LTO and GSIS,
respectively;
c) create an Inventory Committee who shall (i) conduct complete physical
count of PPE; (ii) prepare/update the corresponding RPCPPE (with
appropriate remarks on the condition and whereabouts of the PPE); and
(iii) assess PPEs for any potential impairment using the indications
enumerated under Paragraph 27 of IPSAS 21;
d) order the Municipal Engineer (ME) to submit to the MAO a
monitoring/status report of completed projects at year-end to facilitate the
timely recording of PPE in the books supported with complete documents
as required under pertinent laws; and
e) instruct the concerned department heads to review the requirements and
follow COA Circular No. 2020-006 on the one-time cleansing of PPE in
order to have a verifiable data on the existence, condition and
accountability of the PPEs.

6.20 We recommended the management to:
a) instruct the MA to i) in coordination with the ME, record the cost of
partially completed infrastructure projects at year-end under the CIP
account and the corresponding Accounts Payable thereof; and ii) draw the
adjusting entries to correct the erroneous recording/classification of
completed projects; and
b) ensure the commitment of the MEO to submit to the MAO a monitoring
report/statement of work accomplishment regarding the status/progress of
the construction of all ongoing projects at year-end to facilitate the timely
recording of CIP in the books.

7.16 We recommended that Management:
a) instruct the MA to trace the records/documents relative to the unaccounted
balance of Due to NGAs account amounting to ₱500,204.69, reconcile the
same, and return any unutilized funds for completed/cancelled projects to
the SA; and
b) ensure that all funds received from SAs are supported with duly signed
MOA/TA to clearly define the specific guidelines and procedures in the
implementation of individual projects, including the responsibilities of the
Municipality, thereby ensuring timely accomplishment thereof for the
benefit of the intended beneficiaries.

8.31 We commended the management for ensuring that the outstanding balance of
the TF does not contain unexpended balances that aged for more than five years.
However, we recommended that Management:
a) instruct the MA to draw the adjusting entries to correct the recording of
transactions out of LDRRMF;
b) direct the Acting MT and the MA to facilitate the transfer of funds from
GF to TF for the (i) unexpended balances of QRF and DRRMF-MOOE for
CY 2023 totaling ₱3,181,618.84; and (ii) rehabilitation of the Office of the
MDRRMO erroneously paid out of TF instead of GF amounting to
₱930,650.38;
c) make a representation from the Sangguniang Bayan (SB) for the authority
to open and maintain a separate bank account with an Authorized
Government Depository Bank to secure the STF from co-mingling with the
regular TF;
d) require the MDRRMO to prepare the Report on the Utilization of DRRMF
to be certified correct by the MA and submit to the AT on or before the
15th day after the end of each month; and
e) require the Supply Officer to prepare and submit the SSMI together with
the original copy of the RIS to the MA for issued inventories based on
actual consumption/issuance for accurate recording of expenses and assets
accounts for the period.

9.38 We recommended that Management instruct:
a) the MPDC and the ME together with the Local Development Council and
end-users to (i) ensure that the identified PPAs under 20% DF are well-
planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready; and (ii) conduct
regular monitoring of the status of the project to ensure the full
implementation of all planned PPAs;
b) the BAC to require the winning contractors to (i) submit DOLE-approved
CSHP; and (ii) post-performance security with validity until the issuance
of the Certificate of Final Acceptance and warranty security which runs
for a period of one year from the date of final acceptance of the project;
c) the ME to (i) integrate the CSHP in the computation of construction costs
of all projects to be implemented by the Municipality; and (ii) monitor the
proper implementation of CSHP of the contractors, particularly the
provision of personal protective equipment to construction workers, to
ensure its compliance with the requirements of the DOLE; and
d) all winning contractors to submit their respective CSHP to DOLE for
approval before the implementation of each infrastructure project.

9.39 We also reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that Management
consider hiring additional engineers or technical personnel to help with the
preparation of detailed engineering and PoWs, if necessary, to avoid unjustifiable
delays in the implementation of development projects.

9.40 Management acknowledged the audit observations and committed to comply with
the audit recommendations.

10.27 We recommended that Management, through the SWM Board, undertake the
following courses of action:
a) review the SWMP immediately and identify areas for updating and/or
revisions to ensure its sustainability, viability, effectiveness, and relevance
in relation to local and international developments in the field of SWM;
b) immediately facilitate the dumping of solid wastes piled up at the
Municipal MRF to the sanitary landfill;
c) prioritize the repair of the shredder and start using the bio-composter to
maximize its use and to avoid further damage to the equipment; and
d) on the implementation of the Comprehensive ESWM ordinance: (i)
educate the public on the proper waste segregation, collection, and disposal
of wastes through the conduct of continuous forums and seminars; (ii)
comply with the requirements of the law on waste segregation by placing
trash bins with proper labels and by collecting only those garbage that are
properly segregated; (iii) coordinate with the barangay officials and
business establishments in the strict implementation of the existing
environmental law; and (iv) impose the fines and penalties against violators
of prohibited acts to improve compliance rate and provide sufficient
funding for SWM PPAs.

11.39 We recommended the Management to:
a) instruct the Acting MT to (i) prepare a stratified schedule of PIL every tax
period to approximate the gross sales or receipt of each business
classification and submit a representation to the SB to set up or
institutionalize the use of the same; (ii) regularly examine the books of
accounts of business establishments to determine the truthfulness of the
declared gross sales or receipts used in the computation of business taxes
paid pursuant to Section 171 of R.A. No. 7160; (iii) equip the personnel at
the MTO and the BPLS with the necessary knowledge and training in the
examination of books of accounts to enable the said Offices to carry on
with their mandate efficiently; and (iv) tag all the afore-cited suppliers and
contractors with underdeclared sales or receipts and be subject to
examination of books of accounts to ensure the correctness of taxes, fees,
and charges to be collected;
b) advise the LO to require the submission of complete documentation of the
financial records from taxpayers and ensure that these records form part
of their permanent files to easily verify the correctness of the assessments;
and
c) require the Acting MT and the LO to devise measures to validate the
declared gross sales or receipts of establishments applying for business
permits especially those who are having direct transactions with the
Municipality and its Barangays.

12.16 We recommended that management instruct the GFPS to:
a) monitor the implementation of GAD PPAs and evaluate the level and
extent to which the GAD PPAs have become effective and efficient in
addressing GAD issues;
b) use a gender monitoring tool like HGDG on the attributed budget to
evaluate gender responsiveness of PPAs in the GBP;
c) capacitate the concerned officials on the use of HGDG tool through
trainings and seminars; and
d) create a GAD M&E Team to evaluate the outcomes of GAD PPAs.

13.6 We recommended that Management advise the MA to (a) continue to comply
with the BIR rules and regulations on the withholding and remittance of taxes on
compensation and payments of purchases and contracts; and (b) exert extended
efforts to verify and reconcile the balance of Due to BIR account and immediately
effect the necessary adjustments and/or remittance to BIR thereafter.

14.5 We commended the Management for complying with the provisions of the
RIRR of the National Health Insurance Act of 2013 on the proper deduction of
PhilHealth contributions of its personnel and the remittance thereof within the
prescribed period and recommended the continuous observance thereof for the

Municipal Treasurer to (i) prepare a schedule of all unreleased checks every end of the year for submission to the MAO; and (ii) send written notice/s to the payees of the existence of the unclaimed checks at least one month before a check becomes stale; and
Municipal Accountant to (i) make necessary adjustments to record the stale and unreleased checks by debiting CIB-LCCA and crediting Accounts Payable; (ii) closely monitor and check regularly the entries in the CIB accounts to ensure their accuracy; (iii) submit immediately to the Audit Team the BRS for October to December 2021; (iv) henceforth, prepare the bank reconciliation statements immediately to identify reconciling items that will require adjustment in the books and/or verification with the depository bank; and (v) review the staffing requirement of the MAO to determine the appropriate manpower complement and request for additional personnel, if necessary.
The Municipal Accountant stated that the necessary adjusting entries will be made in CY 2022. Management agreed to comply with the other audit recommendations.

We recommended and Management agreed to consider the following remedial measures; to wit:
instruct the Municipal Accountant to (1) exert diligent effort to trace the source of prior years’ unaccounted balance of Land account totaling ₱232,035.11; (2) draw JEVs taking up the recognition of (i) assets procured out of TF under the appropriate Assets accounts in the GF books; (ii) depreciation expenses for completed infrastructure project and PPEs purchased out of TF; and (iii) dropping of obsolete properties from the books upon disposal; and (3) update the PPELC for all property accounts to support the recorded balances of PPE; and (4) henceforth, ensure that the infrastructure projects are recorded to appropriate Asset accounts upon completion;
direct the Property Custodian to (i) maintain complete and updated property cards per category of PPE; and (ii) identify all unserviceable properties for disposal and initiate the disposal of unserviceable properties to realize additional income therefrom for the benefit of the Municipality; and
instruct the Municipal Assessor and Property Custodian to ascertain the status of other parcels of land owned by the Municipality with titling issues and facilitate the titling thereof in the name of the LGU to warrant the absolute and clear ownership;
create an Inventory Committee who shall (i) conduct complete physical count of PPE; and (ii) prepare the corresponding RPCPPE with appropriate remarks on the condition and whereabouts of the PPE; and
form a Disposal Committee who shall perform the immediate disposal all unserviceable and unusable office/other supplies and equipment using appropriate mode of disposal, as determined and recommended by the Committee, to avoid further deterioration and earn income from the sale thereof, if warranted.
The Municipal Accountant agreed to take up in the books of accounts the necessary adjusting entries to correct the PPE accounts. Management committed to comply with the other audit recommendations.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
instruct the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to facilitate the transfer of unexpended LDRRMF for CY 2021 amounting to ₱4,368,455.28 to the TF account to support disaster risk reduction and management activities of the Municipality; and
direct the Municipal Accountant to draw the following adjusting entries to correct the recording of transactions out of LDRRMF:

The Municipal Accountant agreed to book up the necessary adjustments pertaining to LDRRMF transactions.

We commended Management for providing and utilizing necessary resources to respond and eliminate the threat of COVID-19. However, we recommended and Management agreed to:
direct the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council to carefully plan and execute LDRRM-related PPAs to achieve the goals and objective of the fund and the desired benefits; and
make a representation from the Sangguniang Bayan (SB) for the authority to open and maintain a separate bank account with an AGDB to secure the STF from co-mingling with the regular TF.
Management committed to comply with the audit recommendations.

We recommended and Management agreed to instruct the Municipal Health Officer in coordination with the Human Resource Management Officer and the Municipal Accountant to submit the (i) list of eligible recipients for AHDP signed by the Local Health Board; (ii) appointments/plantilla of the subject regular and JO personnel; (iii) duties and responsibilities with duly verified accomplishment reports; and (iv) DTRs to further assess the validity, propriety, and legality of AHDP granted to them.
Management committed to comply with the audit recommendations.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation and Management agreed to instruct the MPDC and the Municipal Engineer (a) to abide strictly with the project schedule by conducting regular monitoring of the status of project implementation; and (b) together with the Local Development Council and end-users to properly plan the PPAs to be included in the 20% Local Development Plan, to ensure the full implementation of all the priority projects targeted for the year for the benefit of the Municipality and the constituents in general.
Management committed to comply with the audit recommendations.

We recommended and Management agreed to (a) direct the Municipal Accountant, the Municipal Treasurer, and the Municipal Budget Officer/BAC Chairman to adhere strictly to the timeframes on the rendition of accounts and preparation/submittal of financial reports, including copies of perfected contracts/purchase orders pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-006 and other pertinent issuances; and (b) submit immediately the aforementioned documents and reports.
The concerned officials committed to comply with the timeframes of submission of accounts and financial reports. Likewise, the perfected contracts/purchase orders will be submitted within five days from the execution of these documents.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
instruct the GFPS through the Municipal Planning and Development Officer to establish a GAD database to include gender statistics and sex-disaggregated data to serve as inputs for planning, budgeting, programming, and policy formulation;
strive for the full implementation of GAD-related PPAs by utilizing judiciously the budget set aside for said purposes; and
ensure that the GAD Accomplishment Report is supported with required documents to provide the basis of measuring the performance of the LGU in terms of all the requirements of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.
The designated GAD Focal Person stated that they will start to set-up and maintain GAD Database to serve as basis for gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation. She averred that the low implementation rate of GAD-related PPAS was due to the persisting effect of COVID-19 outbreak which restricts physical contact.

We recommended and Management agreed to instruct the:
BAC, together with its Technical Working Group and Secretariat to (i) comply with the required period in posting the NOA and NTP; (ii) require the contractor to post performance security with validity until the issuance of the Certificate of Final Acceptance and warranty security which runs for a period of one year from the date of final acceptance of the project; and (iii) scrutinize thoroughly the documents submitted by the bidders to ensure their authenticity, veracity, and completeness as the basis for the evaluation of the most advantageous and reasonable bids; and
Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the BAC and Municipal Engineer for the completion and submission of the noted documentary deficiencies and ensure that only disbursement vouchers with complete documentation are processed and presented for payment.
Management committed to comply with the audit recommendations.

We recommended and Management agreed to instruct the MAO and MTO to (a) put on extra effort to verify the unaccounted balance of ₱185,994.46 and remit the same; and (b) maintain their good practice of religiously observing the prescribed deadlines on the withholding of premiums/deductions and the respective remittance thereof to the GSIS in pursuance of the GSIS Act of 1997.
The Municipal Accountant admitted that they encountered difficulty in tracing the previous years’ unaccounted balance due to absence of necessary records and documents. Nevertheless, Management committed to comply with the audit recommendations.

We commended the efforts of the MAO and MTO and recommended to continue to be compliant with regulations on withholding and remittance of monthly PhilHealth contributions within the prescribed period.

We appreciated the effort of Management for full remittance of premiums and loan amortizations to Pag-IBIG during the year. However, for the remittances not equal to the amount withheld, we recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to ensure prompt submission of liquidation/payrolls/DVs to the MAO for timely recording of the same on the books of accounts.
Management committed to comply with the audit recommendations.

We recommended and Management agreed to (a) advise the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to continually comply with the withholding and full remittance of the taxes withheld within the prescribed period; and (b) instruct the personnel in-charge to (i) reconcile regularly the Due to BIR account and effect necessary adjustments immediately for any discrepancy noted; (ii) draw the journal entries under the GF to recognize the receivables from Other Funds amounting to ₱137,203.01 for the withholding taxes remitted thru GF by debiting Due from Other Funds and crediting Due to BIR account; and (iii) include in the succeeding remittance the unremitted withholding taxes of ₱22,708.48 as at year-end.
The Municipal Accountant will ensure that the amount of remittance is reconciled with the withholding taxes.  Management committed to comply with other audit recommendations.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that Management exhaust all available remedies provided by law and regulations in enforcing the immediate settlement of the audit disallowances totaling ₱11,431,027.19.

We recommended that the MDRRMO submit the Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances together with the required supporting documents to the NDRRMC through the OCD, copy furnish the Audit Team within 10 working days after the end of each quarter pursuant to COA Circular No. 2020-009 in order for the Audit Team and the oversight agencies to conduct timely evaluation of the receipt and utilization of the aids and donations received by the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: The MDRRMO submitted Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances together with the required supporting documents to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation:
1.5 We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to instruct
the Municipal Accountant to:
a) draw the adjusting entries to correct the misstatements of the Property,
Plant and Equipment (PPE) on (i) reclassification of semi-expendable
items to Inventory accounts; and (ii) recognition of assets purchased out
of BGCM totaling to ₱407,900.00;
b) make the necessary adjustments to correct the accumulated depreciation
recorded as of year-end for the aforementioned semi-expendable items
costing below ₱15,000.00 which were inappropriately recorded as PPEs
and unrecognized Inventory and PPE items purchased out of BGCM
funds, if any; and
c) henceforth, ensure that (i) semi-expendable items costing below
₱15,000.00 are classified to the appropriate Inventory/Expenses accounts
for accurate and fair presentation of the PPE accounts in the financial
statements; and (ii) Assets/Expenses charged from grants and the
corresponding Income were recognized in the books of accounts.

2.3 We recommended and the LCE agreed to consider the following measures:
a) instruct the Municipal Accountant to (i) draw JEVs taking up the
issued/consumed supplies and materials at year-end; and (ii) maintain
supplies ledger cards for all inventory accounts to support the recorded
balances of inventory; and
b) require the Municipal Treasury and/or Supply Officer to (i) prepare the
stock cards for the purchased supplies; (ii) conduct periodic inventory of
supplies as prescribed by the above regulation; (iii) prepare the
corresponding Report on the Physical Count of Inventories and submit the
same to the Accounting Office for reconciliation with the reported inventory
balances in the books; and (iv) prepare and submit to the Accounting Office
the SSMIs monthly for issued inventories based on actual
consumption/issuance for accurate recording of expenses and assets accounts
for the period.

3.6 We recommended and the LCE agreed to:
a. instruct the OIC-Budget Officer to submit the SB
Resolution/Ordinance/Supplemental Budget showing the source of funds
amounting to ₱537,750.00 for the payment of HP, charged to MOOE:
b. explain/justify, together with the Legislative Body, why the agency failed
to grant a uniform rate of COVID-19 HP per person to all qualified
personnel;
c. advise the Human Resource Management Office to submit, if any, the
required (i) order authorizing the employees to physically report for
work; and (ii) copy of the duties and responsibilities of the recipients
during the period of grant;
d. instruct the Municipal Accountant to (i) submit the logbook and
Accomplishment Reports of the aforementioned personnel; and (ii) to
explain why some JOs were able to claim more or less than what was
allowed per DTRs; and
e. advise the Immediate Supervisor of 16 JO employees to refund excess
claims totaling ₱52,950.00.

4.18 We recommended and the LCE agreed to undertake the following courses of
action:
a. instruct the MSWDO, in close coordination with the Barangay officials
concerned and the DSWD, to organize a validation team who will conduct
verification on the eligibility of the aforementioned cases based on the
encoded SAC forms;
b. direct the validation team to submit to the Audit Team a report on the
result of their verification and the actions taken by management and the
liable persons thereon; and
c. advise the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the MSWDO, to
(i) submit the copies of accomplished SAC Forms of beneficiaries; (ii)
ensure that all disbursement vouchers are properly supported with
complete documentation before making any payments; and (iii) liquidate
immediately, the remaining ₱45,500.00 refund of 7 beneficiaries to
DSWD Regional Office No. 3.

5.8 We recommended and the LCE agreed to (a) require the BAC to prepare
revised APP in the prescribed format to cover succeeding emergency procurements;
b) instruct the Municipal Accountant to submit the deficient documents mentioned
in Appendix A of this report; (c) ensure that the reporting and posting requirement
of succeeding grant and fund transfer will be strictly adhered to ; and (d) prior to
liquidation/return of any unexpended balance of fund transfers from NGAs,
communicate/clarify, as the circumstances may warrant, to the concerned NGAs,
any misinterpretation or deviation from prescribed guidelines to maximize the use
of the said funds.

6.7 We recommended and the LCE agreed to :
(a) direct the MDRRMC to carefully plan and execute LDRRM-related
PPAs to achieved the goals and objective of the fund and the desired
benefits, from preparation of LDRRMIP to project implementation;
(b) instruct the Municipal Accountant to set-up the aforementioned
correcting entry;
(c) instruct the Municipal Accountant and Bids and Awards Committee
(BAC) to (i) submit the aforementioned required documents
immediately to the Office of the Auditor for proper/further evaluation of
the transactions and (ii) comply with the required reportorial
requirements of R. A. No. 9184 for information purposes and instruct
the MDRRM Officer and Municipal accountant to ensure timely
preparation of the Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF on or
before the 15th day after the end of each month and submit to the Office
of the Auditor within the aforesaid reglementary period; and
(d) Stop granting of cash advances for meal expenses that deviates proper
procurement procedures and submit legal basis for the grant of meal
allowances charged under the LDRRMF.

7.7 We recommended and the LCE agreed to:
a. instruct the MPDC-OIC and the Municipal Engineer to conduct regular
monitoring of the status of project implementation to ensure timely
execution of PPAs; and
b. instruct the Municipal Engineer to (i) coordinate with the contractors to
comply with the required posting of information and publicity on PPAs of
government agencies; and (ii) inform the Audit Team within ten (10) days
after the award of the project or before the start of the PPA that the
appropriate project signboards and/or public notices are already posted
at the project site.

8.8 We recommended and the LCE agreed to:
a. consider the application of postpaid plans under a corporate/enterprise
account of the Municipality to a chosen telecommunication service
provider for economic reasons, to foster equity in the use of mobile in
relation to actual communication needs of the LGU and for ease in
monitoring and control of mobile expenses;
b. thru the assistance of the SB, establish clear-cut guidelines for the use of
fund in Telephone Expenses-Mobile by the officials and employees; and
c. instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that the required
supporting documents for Telephone Expenses-Mobile are attached to
the DV to support the validity of the claims.

9.8 We recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant,
the Municipal Treasurer, the Municipal Budget Officer, and the BAC Chairman to
(a) adhere strictly to the timeframes on the rendition of accounts and
preparation/submittal of financial reports, including copies of perfected
contracts/purchase orders; and (b) see to it that all unsubmitted accounts (vouchers
and official receipts) for prior years and CY 2020 are transmitted to the Office of
the Auditor, including the original disbursement vouchers and/or supporting
documents corresponding to the previously submitted photocopied documents;
otherwise, we shall be constrained to recommend the withholding of salaries of
concerned Municipal officials/personnel.

10.8 We recommended and the LCE agreed to:
a) gather GAD information such as gender statistics and sex-disaggregated
data to serve as inputs for planning, budgeting, programming and policy
formulation for the setting up and maintenance of GAD database;
b) create a Monitoring and Evaluation Team to take responsibility of
assessing the status of PPAs in relation to the plan and existing GAD
policy;
c) strive for the full implementation of GAD-related PPAs so that the
desired outcomes are attained and benefits are enjoyed by the intended
beneficiaries; and
d) ensure that the GAD Accomplishment Report is supported with required
documents to provide basis of measuring the performance of the LGU in
terms of all the requirements of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint
Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 dated July 18, 2013.

11.9 We recommended and the LCE agreed to (a) continually abide with the BIR
regulation on the deadline of remittance of taxes withheld from employees as well as
from suppliers/contractors; (b) instruct the personnel in-charge to determine the
exact amount to be remitted and determine the causes of unreconciled amount to
make the necessary adjustments in order to ensure accuracy of ledger balances of
the Due to BIR account per fund.

12.7 We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the Accounting Office to
(a) continuously reconcile remittances with the recorded premiums and loan
deductions and exert extra effort to establish the unreconciled amount to arrive at
accurate amounts of remittances and balance of the account Due to GSIS; and (b)
strictly comply with the prescribed period provided in the GSIS Act on remittances
of contributions and loans to prevent the occurrence of penalty/interest.

13.6 We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant
to strictly adhere to the Pag-IBIG prescribed regulations by remitting regularly in
full the monthly deductions of contributions and employees’ loan amortizations to
avoid paying interest/penalty. Make representation to Pag-IBIG to discuss on how
to make remittances easy on the part of the agency, aside from doing it over the
counter; thus, avoiding the risks involved in the present set-up of remitting the
contributions/loans. Further, exert effort to trace the negative balance of ₱406.21
and make necessary adjustments, if any.

14.6 We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant
to strictly adhere to PhilHealth Circular No. 0001 by remitting regularly and in full
all the monthly deductions made from employees in accordance with the payment
schedule provided so as to avoid imposition of interest charges.

 submit the monthly monitoring report in compliance with the aforementioned

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that management:
instruct the Municipal Accountant to exhaust all means to determine the details and information of the five categories of PPE, such as School Buildings, Machinery, Agricultural and Forestry Equipment, Construction and Heavy Equipment, and Printing Equipment and compute/record the corresponding depreciation for each depreciable asset;
draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) under the General Fund to take up accumulated depreciation of Agricultural and Forestry Equipment and Printing Equipment by debiting Depreciation Expense and Prior Period Adjustment accounts in the amount of ₱666,351.38 and ₱181,323.33, respectively, and crediting Accumulated Depreciation for ₱847,674.71; and
prepare the necessary adjusting entries to (i) record the procurement of collapsible stretcher and Small Water Impounding Projects to their proper accounts; and (ii) recognize the unrecorded assets in the books.

We recommended and management agreed to (a) instruct the Municipal Treasurer to furnish the Municipal Accountant of a duly certified list showing the names of taxpayers and the amounts due and collectible for the year to serve as basis of recording; and (b) direct the Municipal Accountant to (i) make use of the Certified List of Taxpayers and Taxes Due to be submitted by the Municipal Treasurer as basis for setting up the RPT/SET Receivables and Deferred Credits at the beginning of the year; and (ii) trace and record the adjustments to reconcile the differences between the amount of the recorded RPT and SET Receivables and the related Deferred Credits accounts.
The Municipal Treasurer committed to provide the certified list of taxpayers and taxes due to the Municipal Accountant to serve as basis for the recording of RPT/SET Receivables.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive:
instruct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to (i) ensure the completeness of all information and supporting documents submitted by bidders during the evaluation of technical documents and eligibility check including those related to their relevant experience on similar undertakings; and (ii) submit the succeeding contracts and all supporting documents thereto for auditorial review by the Audit Team within five working days from execution pursuant to Section 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009; and
advise the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the BAC for the completion and submission of the documentary deficiencies of the 13 projects and ensure that only disbursement vouchers with complete documentation are processed and presented for payment.
The BAC representative commented that they already started complying with some of the audit recommendations for the projects in CY 2019. They also committed to observe the allowed period for every procurement stage/activity.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendations that the Local Chief Executive:
instruct the BAC and the Municipal Engineer to plan effectively and schedule properly all procurement activities pertaining to development projects to ensure timely implementation and completion thereof; and
direct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and the Municipal Engineer to monitor regularly the status of project implementation so that necessary interventions may be introduced at the earliest time. Likewise, make sure that the funds are properly allocated to development projects that will contribute to the attainment of desirable socio-economic and environmental management outcomes.
The Municipal Engineer commented that some of the unimplemented projects are already implemented in calendar year 2019. They also committed to allot funds for environmental management projects next year.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
instruct the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) for the completion and submission of the documentary deficiencies noted in the disbursement vouchers listed in Appendix H; and
ensure that all disbursement claims for future transactions are supported with complete documentation before payments are made.
Management committed to comply with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the:
Municipal Budget Officer to appropriate and set aside not less than 5% of the estimated revenue from regular sources for MDRRMF;
MDRRMO to (i) monitor and evaluate the utilization of MDRRMF based on the programs, projects and activities listed in the Work and Financial Plan; (ii) ensure compliance with the prescribed minimum standards for disaster preparedness developed by the DILG and LGA; (iii) ensure that the fund intended for post-disaster activities are used in accordance with the provisions of NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1; and (iv) furnish the Office of the Auditor with Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF in the required format through the LDRRMC and Local Development Council on or before the 15th day after the end of each month; and
Municipal Accountant to (i) perform a thorough review of all disbursement claims to ensure that these are properly supported with complete documentation and proper approval by authorized officials/representatives; and (ii) ensure that succeeding transactions have been properly recorded to appropriate expense accounts.
The Municipal Budget Officer commented that they excluded the income from economic enterprises in the computation of the MDRRMF. On the other hand, the LDRRMO commented that they already have MOA with some of the store owners in San Narciso regarding procurement of relief goods and medicines in times of calamity. They promised to provide the Audit Team with the copies of these MOA.

We recommended that management instruct the:
Local School Board to judiciously plan the priority PPAs to be included in the LSB Plan and Budget to avoid several realignment of funds;
Municipal Accountant/Municipal Treasurer to (i) submit explanation on the discrepancies noted on the procurement documents, as summarized in Appendix K; and (ii) combine efforts to submit immediately the lacking documents enumerated in Appendix L;
Prepare necessary adjusting entries to correct the erroneous recording of the purchase uniforms amounting to ₱32,080.25 by debiting Other Supplies and Materials Expense and crediting Food Supplies Expenses; and
Municipal Accountant to ensure that succeeding disbursements charged against the Fund are properly supported with complete documentation to avoid incurrence of audit suspensions/disallowances.
The PSDS, Co-Chair of Local School Board commented that four resolutions were made on the realignment of SEF due to (i) immediate need and completion of some projects such as Kitchenette with Comfort Room-Phase II which was supposedly funded by the barangay but did not materialize, (ii) fund for kinder facilitators was realigned due to deployment of permanent kindergarten teachers, and (iii) increase in the number of participants in the Athletic Meet and great number of education research activity. There were also changes of projects because the proposed projects were already funded by the DepEd.
Erasures on the dates of the documents were made by the Office during the checking of submitted documents due to erroneous filling up of dates. On the other hand, sometimes only photocopy of the document was attached due to limited original copies particularly the Project Proposal. Nonetheless, they committed to comply with the deficiencies in the documentation and promised to be more careful next time.
The Municipal Accountant suggested to prepare additional original documents to prevent submission of photocopies only.

We recommended that management instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer, including all Accountable Officers to:
immediately transmit to the Audit Team the unsubmitted accounts and financial reports including the original disbursement vouchers and/or supporting documents of the previously submitted photocopied transactions;
strictly comply to the timeframes on the rendition of accounts and preparation/submission of financial reports; and
impose appropriate sanctions against those officials/employees concerned who have continuously disregarded the set deadline for the submission of the accounts/reports.
The Municipal Accountant commented that as regards to the prior years’ unsubmitted accounts, all original documents were already submitted to COA and only photocopies remained in their file. Nevertheless, they will double check again.

We recommended that management:
prioritize the repair of the unserviceable machineries, equipment and vehicles and consider purchasing additional units to cope with the collection, transport and disposal of solid wastes;
continuously educate the constituents, particularly the market vendors, on the proper waste segregation and implement the “No segregation, no collection policy” more strictly;
intensify the information and education campaign to stop totally the open burning of solid wastes, particularly in the rural and remote areas; and
expedite the process of promulgation of local ordinance prohibiting the open burning of solid wastes like hay stalks, dried leaves and other farm wastes and set fines and penalties for violators.
The SWM Coordinator commented that a request was already made for another plastic pulverizer to accommodate the big volume of plastic, dried leaves and vegetable being collected. They assured the Audit Team that the unserviceable plastic pulverizer will be repaired. Likewise, they are now processing the implementation of giving sanctions with corresponding charges to those who will violate the policy on waste segregation. Notice to explain will be sent to the Punong Barangay of two barangays caught with evidences of burned wastes.
The Municipal Mayor commented that they already started the process of procurement of dump truck intended for hauling of solid wastes.

We recommended that management:
instruct the Municipal Treasurer to exert more effort to (i) intensify revenue collection strategies such as periodic sending of notices/demand letters to all delinquent stallholders/lessees; (ii) enforce the revocation of the lease contract and forfeit in favor of the Municipality any and all improvements found in the stalls of the delinquent lessees; and (iii) avail of all legal and administrative remedies allowed under R.A. No. 7160; and
execute Contract of Lease with the market stallholders, renew those expired contracts with lessees of Municipal-owned buildings, and strictly implement the terms and conditions stated thereto.

The stalls of stall/booth owners who will fail to settle their unpaid accounts until June 30, 2019 will be closed. New contracts will be issued upon transfer to the new public market which is currently under construction. The delinquent stallholders will not be allowed to have stalls in the new public market.
According to the Municipal Treasurer, only two lessees of Municipal-owned buildings remained delinquent. The Market Administrator commented that four delinquent stallholders in the public market already settled their accounts.

We recommended that management expedite the tracing of necessary information and complete the required documents to facilitate the preparation of the request for the disposal of unserviceable properties to COA.

He went to LTO to trace any available records relative to the unserviceable vehicles for disposal but only a few information were gathered.

We recommended and management agreed to (a) continuously monitor receivables by sending demand letters on the delinquent accounts and intensify efforts on collection to increase RPT and SEF collection efficiency; and (b) avail of all legal and administrative remedies either by administrative action through levy on real property or judicial action as a last resort.
The Municipal Treasurer assured us that they will continue to send demand letters to delinquent real property owners.

We recommended that management instruct the Municipal Accountant to (a) ensure that all taxes withheld from employees as well as from suppliers/contractors are immediately remitted to the BIR within the prescribed period; and (b) exercise extra care in the recording of transactions to ensure accuracy of ledger balances of the Due to BIR account per fund.
The Municipal Accountant commented that he already instructed the concerned Accounting personnel to check and tally the remittances to the amount withheld. In case the amount did not tally, they will trace the differences.

We recommended that management continually exert effort to comply with the provisions of Republic Act (R.A.) No. 8291 and its Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations on the withholding and remittance of premium contributions and loan amortizations of employees.
The Municipal Accountant commented that he already instructed the concerned Accounting personnel to check and reconcile the remittances to the amount withheld. In case the amount did not tally, they will trace the differences.

We recommended that management continue to adopt the good practice of promptly remitting premiums, loan amortizations, and other contributions to the Home Development Mutual Fund in compliance with the provisions of Republic Act No. 9679.
The Municipal Accountant commented that he already instructed the concerned Accounting personnel to check and reconcile the remittances to the amount withheld. In case the amount did not tally, they will trace the differences.

We recommended that management strive to (a) comply strictly with the provisions of Republic Act No. 10606 and its Implementing Rules and Regulations on the withholding and remittance of PhilHealth premium contributions; and (b) exert more effort in the analysis of the long outstanding balance of ₱21,121.00.
The Municipal Accountant commented that he already instructed the concerned Accounting personnel to check and reconcile the remittances to the amount withheld. In case the amount did not tally, they will trace the differences.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that management exhaust all available remedies provided by law and regulations in enforcing the immediate settlement of the audit disallowances totaling to ₱11,431,027.19 pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009 otherwise known as the 2009 Rules and Regulations on the Settlement of Accounts (RRSA).
The Municipal Accountant committed to submit the breakdown of settlement thru salary deduction of the NDs relative to payment of excess PEI and financial assistance to Municipal employees and JOs charged to 5% Calamity Fund for issuance of corresponding settlement.

will finish the conduct of physical count of PPEs, after-which, require the Municipal
Treasurer to prepare the Property Card for each category of PPE, in compliance
with Sections 124 and 119 of the MNGAS, Volume 1, to ascertain existence and
valuation of the property and establish accountability thereon.

a. the Municipal Treasurer to immediately liquidate/settle his cash advances in
compliance with existing regulation, otherwise, we will be constraint to file
appropriate administrative/legal action against him;
b. other accountable officers to settle their cash advances by deducting the
corresponding amounts from the money due them; and
c. henceforth, see to it that all cash advances are liquidated at the end of the year
for fair presentation of the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to file the request for write-off of the
accounts to the COA in pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to
make the necessary adjusting entries to record the four breedable female carabaos
as Biological Assets – Breeding Stocks in compliance with Section 112 of PD 1445
and PPSAS 27 for proper presentation in the financial statement.
Also, require the Municipal Agriculturist to prepare the Property Card for the
Biological Assets and to update it regularly; and prepare the Inventory of Biological
Assets for submission to the Accounting Office for recording in the books of
accounts the gain or loss on fair value of biological assets and the addition or
reduction of the same.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to
