observations.recommendation:
1.17 We recommended that the management:
a. mobilize additional competent personnel from other departments to
facilitate the completion of the physical inventory of all properties owned
by the Municipality and report the results thereof using the Report on the
Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE);
b. require the Municipal Treasurer to exert possible remedies in locating the
Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment (ARE) issued in prior years
and ensure the issuance/renewal of corresponding ARE or PAR whenever
the property is issued to the end-user. This should be regularly updated
at least every three years or upon change of property accountability, such
as during transfer and return/surrender of property;
c. instruct the Municipal Accountant to update and reconcile the Property,
Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards (PPELC) against the balances
recorded in the general ledger; and
d. advice the Municipal Treasurer to maintain property cards for each class
of PPE to be regularly reconciled with the PPELC and other pertinent
Accounting records.

2.8 We recommended that the (a) Municipal Accountant (i) maintain the
practice of sending notices or reminders to personnel for the prompt liquidation of
their cash advances as soon as the purpose thereof had been accomplished; (ii) stop
the practice of granting new cash advances without the liquidation of previous ones;
and (iii) submit immediately the lacking documents for various transactions post-
audited and ensure that liquidation reports are supported with complete
documentations.

3.5 We recommended that:
a. the Municipal Treasurer submit a Certified List of Taxpayers with
account balances and complete aging schedule of receivables as of
December 31, 2016 in accordance with Section 20, Volume I of the New
Government Accounting (NGAS) for Local Government Units;
b. instruct the Municipal Accountant to trace and record the adjustments
to reconcile the differences between the amount of the recorded RPT and
SET receivables and the related Deferred Credits accounts;
c. the personnel in-charge in the Municipal Treasurer’s Office regularly
update and reconcile her records of delinquencies with the Real Property
Tax Account Register/Taxpayer’s index card to report the correct
amount of delinquent taxes;
d. the Municipal Treasurer ensure continuous/proper monitoring of
receivables by sending demand letters especially on the delinquent
accounts and intensify efforts on the collection drive towards an
improved RPT and SET collection efficiency; and
e. the Municipal Mayor, through the Municipal Treasurer, avail of all legal
and administrative remedies either by administrative action through levy
on real property or judicial action as provided by Sections 183, 256, 257
to 258 and 270 of the Local Government Code of 1991.

6.13 We recommended that the management ensure that all its insurable assets
are adequately covered by insurance with the GSIS to obtain maximum protection
of the interests of the government and contribute to revenue collection of the
National Government as well.

7.9 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal
Treasurer:
a. strictly comply with the regulation on the rendition of accounts and
financial reports in order not to delay the verification of accounts and
conduct of post-audit activities;
b. observe the due dates of submission of the required financial reports and
documents to the Audit Team; and
c. forward immediately to the Audit Team the unsubmitted 61
disbursement vouchers together with the supporting documents.

9.16 We reiterated the prior year audit recommendations that the Local Chief
Executive (i) instruct the BAC members and the Municipal Engineer to properly
plan and schedule all procurement activities pertaining to development projects to
pave the way for their timely implementation and completion; (ii) ensure that the
Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and the Municipal Engineer
regularly monitor the status of project implementation so that necessary
interventions may be introduced at the earliest time, for instance, deployment of
personnel to provide technical and clerical support, thus, facilitating engineering
activities; and (iii) instruct the Municipal Accountant to cause the immediate
submission of the lacking documentary requirements and ensure that only
disbursement vouchers with complete documentations are processed and presented
for payment to establish proper accountability among certifying/approving agency
officials.

10.14 We recommended that the:
a. Local School Board (i) ensure that only the authorized priority
expenditures identified under the SEF mandate are included and
disbursed in accordance with the SEF Annual Budget and prevent the
incurrence of improperly charged expenditures; and (ii) make sure that
priority SEF projects are implemented and funds utilized exclusively for
the efficient and timely delivery of targeted services and benefits to the
constituents; and
b. Municipal Accountant ensure that disbursements charged against the
Fund are properly supported with complete documentation and approval
of authorized officials/representatives.

11.23 We recommended that the management:
a. maximize the utilization of LDRRMF to ensure the delivery of
calamity-related programs, projects and activities. Likewise, consider
the procurement of early warning systems, preparedness equipment
and other equipage for floods, earthquake, volcanic eruptions, landslide
and other natural and man-made calamities out of the 70% Mitigation
Fund;
b. instruct the Municipal Accountant to perform a thorough review of all
disbursement claims to ensure that these are properly supported with
complete documentation and proper approval by authorized
officials/representatives;
c. advise the Municipal Budget Officer to include any unexpended balance
of DRRMF-CO in the continuing balance under General Fund until the
projects are completed and to comply with the provisions of COA
Circular No. 2012-002 in establishing Registry of Appropriations,
Allotments and Obligations for Quick Response Fund, Disaster Risk
Reduction and Management Fund-Maintenance and Other Operating
Expenses, Disaster Risk Reduction and Management FundCapital
Outlay for proper monitoring of funds and to establish the correct
balance to be transferred to Special Trust Fund. Such balances should
be submitted to the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer for
recording and issuance of check;
d. instruct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to
include in the LDRRMFIP the list of programs, projects and activities
to be undertaken out of the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years;
and
e. consider the creation of permanent position for LDRRM Officer and
hire additional staff to comply with the required three (3) technical staff
responsible for administration and training; research and planning; and
operations and warning.

12.19 We recommended that the management:
a. coordinate with the Department of Environment and Natural Resources
(DENR) in locating and acquiring a suitable lot for the construction of
sanitary landfill and secure Environmental Compliance Certificate
(ECC) before operation. Likewise, seek possible assistance from the
national government for funding the proposed Sanitary Landfill;
b. ensure that waste segregators are equipped with personal protective
equipment to free them from hazards of handling solid wastes;
c. encourage the active participation of schools, private organizations and
the barangay officials in informing and educating households in the
proper waste disposal from source;
d. consider purchasing additional machinery, equipment and vehicle
necessary for the collection, transport and disposal of solid wastes;
e. ensure that the existing Materials Recovery Facility (MRF) of the
component barangays are fully functional and provide necessary support
in enjoining the remaining barangays without MRF to establish their
own;
f. prohibit the open burning of solid wastes like hay stalks, dried leaves and
other farm wastes to protect air and water from pollution; and
g. prioritize the implementation of programs and projects identified in the
SWMP and AIP pertaining to solid waste management.

13.20 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. ensure that at least 5% of the total LGU annual appropriations is allotted
to support GAD PAPs;
b. see to it that the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) undertake its function
of conducting gender mainstreaming to generate gender-responsive plans
and programs;
c. make sure that GAD information are reliable such as, gender statistics
and sex-disaggregated data, which shall serve as inputs for planning,
budgeting, programming and policy formulation for the setting up and
maintenance of GAD database;
d. strive for the full implementation of GAD-related PAPs by maximizing
the application of available funds for the intended purposes exclusively so
that the desired outcomes are attained and benefits are enjoyed to the
fullest by the intended beneficiaries; and
e. evaluate the GAD plan, budget and accomplishments periodically or as
frequently as needed to ascertain whether the activities undertaken by the
LGU effectively addressed the real gender issues and concerns of men
and women within the locality.

14.10 We recommended that the:
a. Monitoring Committee strictly comply with the full disclosure policy
requirements specifically for the required period and modalities of
posting the financial documents in at least three conspicuous places of the
Municipality and in the Full Disclosure Policy Portal pursuant to DILG
Memorandum Circular No. 2013-140;
b. MIS in-charge expedite the creation/development of an LGU website
readily accessible to the public to ensure the quick delivery of
information about the sources and utilization of public funds to the
Municipalitys stakeholders; and
c. Municipal Engineer and Municipal Planning and Development
Coordinator (i) strictly comply with the mandatory posting of
information and publicity on programs/projects/activities of the
Municipal Government and (ii) inform the Audit Team within ten days
after the award of any project or before the start of the
program/project/activity that the appropriate project signboards and/or
public notices are already posted at the project site to facilitate
validation/inspection.

15.6 We recommended that the:
a. Members of the SB consider enacting appropriate ordinance on the
regulation of the operations of three-wheeled public conveyances to
prevent undesirable outcomes as a result of unregulated proliferation of
tricycles along the national highways, which may pose hazards and
imminent danger to pedestrians and motorists lives and properties and
impede the flow of traffic;
b. implement stricter collection system on the payment of franchise and
other fees and consider imposing higher penalty to deter increase of
violations;
c. Municipal Human Resource Officer consider the necessity of hiring
additional traffic enforcers assigned in two-shifts, that would apprehend
and impose fines and penalties to traffic violators; and
d. consider installing Closed Circuit Televisions (CCTVs) to monitor the
situation in the locality including safety and traffic conditions in major
thoroughfares within the Municipality; facilitate tracking and
investigation of violators; and provide legislators (SB) with situational
updates for improving existing ordinances.

16.14 We recommended that the management: (a) ensure that all taxes withheld
from employees as well as from suppliers/contractors were immediately remitted to
the BIR within the prescribed period; and (b) Accounting personnel in-charge
should exert more efforts in determining the details of the unremitted taxes
particularly the prior year’s balances and immediately remit the same to the BIR.

17.9 We recommended that the management exhaust all available remedies
provided by law and regulations in enforcing the immediate settlement of the audit
disallowances totaling to ₱11,431,027.19 pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-006
dated September 15, 2009 otherwise known as the 2009 Rules and Regulations on
the Settlement of Accounts (RRSA).

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor create a
committee that will conduct physical count of PPEs, after-which, require the
Treasurer to prepare the Property Card for each category of assets, in
compliance with Sections 119 and 124 of the Manual on NGAS, Volume
1, to ascertain existence and valuation of the property and establish accountability
thereon.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require:

a. the Municipal Treasurer to immediately liquidate/settle his cash
advances in compliance with existing regulation or else we will be
constraint to file appropriate administrative/legal action against him;

b. Also, other accountable officers to settle their cash advances

otherwise, deduct the corresponding amounts from the money due
them; and

¢. Henceforth, see to it that all cash advances are liquidated at the end

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor make

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the

We also recommended the imposition of sanctions to erring officials

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor send demand leiter to
QUEZELCO for the collection of the loan or make an arrangement for the possible
off-setting of the amount from the bill for electric consumption of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor make representation with
the Sangguniang Bayan to pass a resolution authorizing her to request the

® Municipal Accountant to verify first the reconciling items to ensure that

e Municipal Treasurer to deposit immediately the amount of

observations.managementAction:
CY 2015 AAR | Management hired a Barangay Solid Waste Management Coordinator in-charge of coordinating with the barangays regarding the establishment of MRF.








Orientation-seminar on solid waste management already conducted. The
Barangay Solid Waste Management Coordinator coordinated with various school heads for proper implementa-tion of the program.
observations.reason:
At present, the Municipality does not plan to construct a Sanitary Landfill because (i) it does not have the resources to build one since it costs between 30-50 million pesos; and (ii) it does not have available required lot size to build it. Instead, the management has signed up a MOA with the Metro Clark Sanitary Landfill for the proper disposal of local segregated residual wastes.








MRFs were already established in majority of the barangays.








The Municipality has no plan of constructing a Sanitary Landfill.








Lack of active participation by some of the barangay constituents deterred the conduct of information education campaign.








There was already an existing ordinance but not strictly implemented.








Mixed wastes were still received particularly by the Market MRF. The mandatory segregation of wastes at source was not yet actively participated by the majority of the market vendors and commercial/ business establishments. 








There was available fund for the SWMP under the  20% Development Fund, however, the Municipality has incurred very minimal expenditures relative to the implementation of programs and projects on solid waste management.
